what: The real-pg_promote failover proof for examples/live-capstone (ADR 0042 Inc4/Inc8, leg L-45e1b56b, run task L-839c47e8). Converts test/failover.pg.ts from a typechecked-only spec into an executed proof: the timeline-increment resume decision fires against a REAL Postgres timeline bump from pg_promote, not the forged cursor of acceptance.pg.ts assertion 6c.
how: Booted the two-node stack in docker-compose.failover.yml (a primary + a physical streaming-replication standby built via `pg_basebackup -R`) on the OFFICIAL postgres:16 image, then ran `bun test/failover.pg.ts` against the two nodes. The leg captures a real pre-failover (systemId, timeline 1, LSN) resume cursor on the primary, stops+drops that consumer's slot, `pg_promote`s the standby (timeline increments, systemId held), and asserts a reconnecting client with the pre-failover cursor RE-SNAPSHOTS against the new timeline (never a false-continuity replay), with a positive control that a live post-failover cursor replays on the SAME timeline.

result.finalLine: ✅ Tier-4 v1 real-failover leg PASSED — pg_promote's timeline increment drives a real re-snapshot

identityProof.timelineHistoryOnPromotedNode: 00000002.history: `1	0/303E508	no recovery target specified` — the branch record proving timeline 2 forked from timeline 1 at LSN 0/303E508
identityProof.note: The systemId is IDENTICAL pre and post (same cluster), while the timeline incremented 1→2 from a real pg_promote — the exact (systemId-constant, timelineId-incremented) shape a same-cluster failover produces, which a systemId-only resume check would miss. These values are NOT printed in failover-pg.log (the leg's console shows only the 9 pass/fail lines); they were read live from the DB (system_identifier, WAL filename, timeline-history file) and are recorded VERBATIM in the companion failover-pg-identity.txt — captured in the SAME `docker compose up` cycle as the console log, so the two artifacts describe one run. They corroborate the leg's own IDENTIFY_SYSTEM assertions 5/6 from the database side, independently.

premises: The standby is a genuine physical streaming replica, not two unrelated primaries: leg assertion 1 fails CLOSED unless the standby shares the primary's systemId AND is in recovery. (That the standby was actively STREAMING — pg_stat_wal_receiver=streaming from pg-primary — is an additional pre-failover fact recorded in failover-pg-identity.txt; the leg's assertion 1 itself checks only shared systemId + pg_is_in_recovery, which is sufficient to prove a promotable physical replica.)

A promotion is IRREVERSIBLE — a re-run needs the standby rebuilt from a fresh basebackup (`down -v` then `up`), which is why this is a run-once evidence capture. It now also runs in a push/dispatch CI job (live-capstone-failover.yml, L-c052144e); promotion to a per-PR gate once a first GH-hosted run is green is tracked in L-34963d5f.

Single-writer hazard respected: the pre-failover consumer's slot on the primary is dropped and its connection ended BEFORE promote, so no two consumers are ever alive on one slot across the failover (assertion 9 confirms both nodes' slots end at 0 — no orphaned WAL pin).
